comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.1 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. The book above was my forty-third published book and my sixth published novel. When I finished the first of my tentacle novels, The Weird of Hali:  Innsmouth, I had no idea what I was going to do with the really rather odd para-Lovecraftian fictional cosmos that downloaded itself into my head in the process of writing that first novel. I wasn't even sure at first there would be a sequel, but I enjoyed that world too much to set it aside after just one book. That's when quiet, mousy little Jenny Parrish, a minor character from the first novel, started the process that turned her into one of the two main characters of the series.

I don't invent my characters. I meet them in the recesses of my imagination, and they constantly do things that surprise me. (I suspect this will sound like florid psychosis to anyone who isn't a writer of fiction, but it's the way many of us do things.) Jenny's the all time champion at surprising me in a story. She very quietly let me know that she should be the main character of the second book. When I began writing, I had no idea what her backstory was, I didn't know that she was asexual, I had no idea what her connection to the Lovecraftian cosmos was, and I certainly had no idea that she was only half human and would become the greatest sorceress of this age of the world; she took the lead, in much the same way that Trey sunna Gwen did in the writing of Star's Reach, except Trey was about as subtle as a ruinman's iron pry bar and liked to tip back a few beers and talk, while Jenny simply showed me which way to go and let me figure out the rest for myself.

The book duly got finished and saw print. I kept writing, though various confusions got in the way of publishing the others for a while; when they finally got into print, it was all in a lump. If you're interested in this volume of the series, you can get a copy here if you're in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

replies.content: To my mind it's ruled primarily by Neptune, the planet of soaring idealism and clueless delusion, deception and self-deception, the quest for unity that so often turns to self-contradiction and self-undoing. Neptune is the natural ruler of the 12th house of secret enemies and self-defeating behaviors. He is also the Lord of the Great Deep and the planet of mysticism and enlightenment, but it's always problematic to try to ground his influences in an institutional setting!

Especially if said Neptune was in the 12 house?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Spend plenty of time reflecting and journaling to figure out where you most easily get led into deception and self-deception. Talk to a friend or two and ask them for their impressions on the same subject. The rest depends on what your strengths are -- some people can use their intellect to pick apart the habit of self-deception, some people have better luck with intuition, and so on. Knowing how you tend to fool yourself, though, is crucial.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is definitely a thing, and is even pretty well understood in the more mundane self-help/productivity sphere. It looks like most folks max out the amount of focused, challenging mental effort they can do at 2-4 hours a day (and that's the very top - most get nowhere near that). As sdi says, the muscle metaphor is a decent one, because while there's value in being "warmed up" and "staying in a groove," longer sustained bouts can also be more draining.

So, work, translation, the Tibetan Rites, and Letter Mysticism are likely all drawing from the same pool of mental/spiritual/willpower resources, and there's only so much before you tap out. Long practice can render some of these practices more routine, and thus easier, which means they suck up fewer resources, allowing the addition of new practices, but what seems to work on paper is likely very different from what works in practice, especially over the long run.

One factor I've noticed is that these things are highly affected by "friction" (in the Clausewitzian sense of accumulating small, mundane difficulties making even simple things hard). When everything is cruising along smoothly, I might do a daily SOP, meditation, divination, some breathing exercises, a workout, reading, writing for my blog, and so forth, but then something pops up ("oh, my dad needs help moving some stuff" or "huh, one of the girls has a new class to take her to" or whatever), and some of those things start falling off. The longer something has been a habit and the more central it is, the easier it tends to weather such friction, but it can get frustrating.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Relationship between Names and Things.
comments.content:
Dear Sir,
 Thank you for the opportunity to ask you some questions.   I’ve been wondering about something I’ve seen and heard in the references and I had hoped you might expound on the subject given your wide range of experience and training in the relevant matters. This has to do with the assignation of correspondences to include Names with the heavenly spheres we perceive from Earth. I’ve wondered if the same planets we name today were regarded by the ancients as the same name we ascribe to them. For example, did the cult of Saturn revere the planet Saturn and how do I know this? This is particularly interesting with respect to the outer planets who have names from mythology that were assigned recently and consequently there is not a clearly identifiable public link to the ancient mystery religion practitioners. I’ve especially wondered this as these heavenly spheres are said to have (named) Spirits and Intelligences and these could presumably be contacted using the same means. But that requires at least for some methods knowing its proper Name in a given system. Which if someone has given you a system of generating Names this may not be an issue practically any longer but the ontological question remains as now one could generate Names for planets that don’t exist and what would that mean if one contacted a Spirit or Intelligence by that appellation?

Thank you again for the opportunity to ask you questions. I often find value in the discussions you host and I am but one of many silent and grateful beneficiaries.

Jürgen

replies.title: Re: Relationship between Names and Things.
replies.content:
It depends on which ancients you have in mind. Our names for the ancient planets -- Mercury, Venus, etc. -- are derived from the Latin names for the planets: Mercurius, Venus, Mars, Iupiter, Saturnus. Uranus and Neptune, along with the minor planets Ceres and Pluto and the asteroids, were given modern names by astronomers who paid no attention to the astrological and magical dimensions of those bodies. (Uranus should have been called Prometheus, and Neptune should probably have been called Rhea.)

The ancient Greeks called those five planets Hermes, Aphrodite, Ares, Zeus and Cronus; the Babylonians called them Sihtu, Dilbat, Salbatanu, Sagmegar, and Kayyamanu, and so on. All the ancient languages had their own names for them.

How do we know which name went with which planetary bodies? That's easy; when a Babylonian clay tablet says that Dilbat and Sagmegar were conjunct in the sign of Mulallu on such and such a day in the reign of King Hammurabi, any Babylonian dictionary will tell you that allu is the word for "crab," Mulallu (Mul- is the prefix for things in the sky) is the constellation Cancer the Crab, and you can then look up the date in planetary tables and see that yes, Jupiter and Venus were conjunct in Cancer on that day. It helps that the Babylonians in particular wrote down detailed tables of the planets' movements, so you can check each planet's orbital period -- Kayyamanu takes thirty years to go around the zodiac, Sagmegar twelve, and so on.

replies.title: Re: Relationship between Names and Things.
replies.content: Probably Ops instead of Rhea, if they chose Neptune over Poseidon.

replies.title: Re: Relationship between Names and Things.
replies.content:
It tickles me pink that you know prefixes in ancient Babylonian!

—Ms. Krieger

replies.content: I was very interested in Babylonian and Sumerian astrology for a while. I also find the Babylonian language very beautiful and evocative -- names like Salbatanu and Kayyamanu have a real magic to the ear.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Thank you for your time and for these Magic Mondays. It makes a big difference in my life and is a huge resource in my own personal quests for growth and development.

Question 1:
I was hoping to get your insight about what might be happening with a specific type of experience. I noticed it first and most often during martial arts, but have found it in other spheres of life as well. Occasionally when in situations where there isn't time to think or process in the moment and immediate action is required, I find myself reacting with an action or choice of technique that normally would be highly unlikely to work, but ends up working perfectly. And the action is taken with the knowledge that it is going to work. Not the hunch, but the knowledge it is going to/has worked. It doesn't feel like knowing an outcome before it happens. It feels like simultaneously existing in all moments of the event at the same time.

I haven't asked around, but watching the world around me, I'm guessing this is an experience that is not as uncommon as it sounds at first. I was wondering if you had any thoughts or insight as to what might be going on when this happens?

Question 2:
I'm planning on making a set of divination tiles for someone. This is the first time I'll be making magical tools for someone else and I was wondering if there is anything I should be careful about or do differently than when I make magical tools for myself?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The inner dimensions of human consciousness are not stuck in linear time, and so it's quite common for glimpses of the future to pop through this way. That's one of the things included in that slippery word "intuition."

2) If you can get the other person involved in the making, even in a very minor way, that will help attune the tool to them. Other than that, it's the same.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That kind of experience usually comes from having some particular gift or having achieved some initiation centered around the dimension of gravity. That word 'gravity' does a great job of encompassing many of that dimension's complex characteristics, including an amorphous sense of profundity or importance.

You are quite likely to be on the hero path, with some of its consequent embodied attunements. Experiencing strange glimpses of what will work or what matters or what's important, especially related to body reactions or positions, would be par for the course on that path. Most professional athletes, performers, and warriors would probably describe what you're experiencing as having acute awareness of your body's intelligence or trusting your body's innate instincts. They would, of course, be quite correct (after all, that is a common enough side effect of being able to sense what will work or what's important!)

Occultists simply describe that same effect using different terminology, but we're all pointing at the same effect. Hopefully, we're not displacing our focus off the effect and onto our pointing finger, but that does happen far too often. Congratulations on being able to sense what's needed when, without that any of that finger distraction getting in the way.

— Christophe

comments.title: Harmful magic + ghost?
comments.content:
I encountered a very odd and potentially dangerous situation recently, and would very much appreciate your advice. My apologies in advance for the longish text.

My partner and me recently spent two nights on an "adult getaway" in a rental house explicitly intended for couples, booked through an online portal. Since I expected such a place to have some potentially negative residue from prior use, I included it into my standard daily SOP the few days before our arrival by simply putting a sphere around it at the end, too.

When we arrived, the house was a very old townhouse, but its vibes were pleasant enough and eveything felt well, and I didn't think it necessary to do another SOP right there on arrival (a mistake, as you might guess...). The landlady, a young woman in maybe her early twenties, was nice and friendly, albeit with an off-vibe: over-eager, too wound up, and generally seemed to be trying very hard to make us love the place (my partner later said she seemed to him as if high on drugs, although she probably hadn't taken any).

Right from the start in this house, things were very weird: we both felt sluggish and without much drive, any lovemaking was rather lackluster and we both had a hard time getting to the end. We both got physical issues which mostly seemed to be related to stuck or blocked energies. We also had the constant urge to air the house (not easy because of tiny windows and daytime heat), and by the next day the constant urge to go outside for a walk, a cup of coffee, etc (which is most decidedly not what we had come for!). My partner afterwards told me he also had a "ringing" in his ears which he usually experiences "when something energetic goes on".

In hindsight, the atmosphere was stifling, sluggish, congested, and not very good - but it took both of us until sometime on the second day to realize this at least semi-consciously. Before that, I was of the impression that the house was nice and had good vibes etc.

After our stay, our physical issues if anything got worse rather than better, despite me doing daily SOPs for myself. Also, I sat down with my notebook the day after we returned, to sort out the whole strange episode, and started to make notes on what to ask on MM. However, I totally forgot about everything afterwards - about my questions, about the fact that my physical issues had started on this trip, and even that we've been on a trip at all (similarly for my partner). It took a major intervention (by my higher self? my guardian genius?) about a week later to remember things in the first place - and once I well and truly remembered, I had an incredibly lousy day, but now my physical issues seem to be somewhat better (again, similar for my partner, whom I then started talking to).

During the first night, there was another odd occurence on top of all that: In the middle of the night, we both woke up because we heard (and felt) what I assumed was a ghost on the stairs (very loud creaking, the distinct feeling of somebody walking up towards us). It paused when I said something aloud and pushed an improvised protective sphere outwards, and vanished for good when I started doing a SOP in my imagination. I did some protective stuff on the second day: two SOPs again in my imagination; talking to the house and telling it we just want to sleep peacefully; and reinforcing the protective sphere whenever I woke up at night (which was often due to the very lousy mattress). The second night was quiet.

There were also other oddities (e.g. raving reviews on the online booking portal, even though the mattress was truly horrible; my partner noticing another strange "smell" which couldn't be aired out of the house).

As I said, I'd very much appreciate your advice on a few things, as some of this is both above my current skill level and clearly harmful.

My magical chops are limited: SOP (in general, and for specific purposes), natural magic and practical magic like the red bags, and I worship the planetary deities and have a good connection to them. My partner doesn't practice occultism or magic, but knows about my practice and supports it.


1. The most pressing thing, health-wise, is to make sure all connections to whatever that was are well and truly severed, and any potential residue is cleared.

a. To achieve this, will it be enough if I perform one or more dedicated SOPs for each of us, respectively? And how many would you do?

b. If SOPs might not be enough, what else should I/we do?


2. The whole things stinks to high heaven. Back at that house, I had a hard time thinking clearly about it, and my best (muddled) assumption was that the constant use of the place by couples had attracted some predatory entities which were sucking off energy - and even then I couldn't think clearly enough to consider doing something about it. But with the clarity of hindsight, the fact that the house "felt" much better and clearer than it was, plus the subsequent forgetfulness, make me think that this was some kind of nasty magic, probably by the landlady (or a group she belongs to). It seems that there are at least three parts to it: a. something to make the house appear attractive; b. something to suck of people's sexual/life energy; c. something to make them forget about what happened; and d. potentially also something to cause a harmful backlash if people should remember and throw off the spell.

Does this sound like a probable explanation? Or would something else be equally or more likely? (I'm not expecting a certain tele diagnosis, just trying to get a reasonable idea of what we might have been up against.)


3. I can't in good conscience let future visitors run into this. But I'm also not willing to do anything which would be a risk to my safety or that of my family. (As I started writing this text, our fire alarms went off for no discernible reason, which I take to be a warning to be careful on how I proceed.)

Since this is a place for lovers, I've considered petitioning Venus, bringing the whole story before Her, and then leaving it in Her capable hands to deal with this however She sees fit.

Does that sound like a reasonable plan? Or do you have any other suggestions?


The rest of the questions from here on are less urgent, and I'll gladly repost them next week or two if it's getting too much.

4. The being on the stairs in the first night: Back then I assumed it was a ghost, since it seemed to fit the bill (can't say if there was also something visible, though, as I was too chicken to look...). Once I had gotten over the initial scare, it didn't feel malicious to me. In fact, my first clear intuition was "funny - it came out because it wanted to see the people who did the SOPs and cleared up the atmosphere here". But since I've initially been wrong on so many things in this case, I don't trust my judgement here either.

From the description, would you think this was likely a (harmless) ghost? Or potentially something else, maybe related to the other harmful things?


5. Assuming it was indeed some poor (harmless) soul which got stuck there and can't move on: Strictly speaking, it's not my responsibility, but I would like to do them the courtesy to help, to the extent it's doable and safe for me.

I've considered petitioning Mercury/Hermes in His role as psychopomp and ask if He'd be willing to take care of it, but chances are good that person was a Christian in their material incarnation.

Do you think this would be helpful, or is it a bad idea?


6. Apart from physically performing a SOP right on arrival and one or two more per day while there, is there anything else I/we could have done (short of leaving)?


7. In general, and assuming a capable and experienced performer, is it possible to counter this kind of malicious magic with the SOP? Or would this require the bigger guns?


8. I have been wondering if some of our physical issues might have been triggered by the fact that I did the SOPs etc and thus worked against that magic, and also that we doggedly refused to forget/ignore what we were obviously supposed to forget and ignore.

Is it possible that we triggered some kind of backlash which was built into a magical working and thus were hit harder than we otherwise would have been?



Finally, I wanted to let you know that your teachings make a big difference. Without the SOP and various bit and pieces I've learned from you, I wouldn't have been able to handle this situation as well as I did (even though there is definitely still room for improvement ;) ). And while we didn't get out of it unscathed, things could have been a lot worse.

So thanks a lot, not just for any replies you'll care to give, but also for the work you do, and especially for Magic Mondays!

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Numerology & Astrology

replies.title: RJ Stewart
replies.content:
I very much like all RJ Stewart's work, including his fascinating trilogy on "The Sphere of Art" about his approach to spiritual alchemy.

For Faery work, many of his books are relevant but the one which is most specific is called "The Well of Light: From Faery Healing to Earth Healing." It's a very fine book. Stewart's student Orion Foxwood wrote a book that in some ways goes into more depth of detail about faeries and working with them, which Stewart wrote the foreword for. Foxwood's book is "The Faery Teachings," and he's got a sequel to it, as well. Both authors speak of the need for circumspection and respect when dealing with faery beings, and offer ways to do this. Stewart also has a CD of his original music that was composed to assist one in meditatively walking the faery path, as an accompaniment to his book.

I don't work with beings that are described as faery in the more Celtic-oriented traditions, but I do work with beings that in my Heathen tradition are called landwights, housewights, dwarves, giants, and the like. Stewart's book includes among the faery tribes beings that are called otherwise in other traditions such as giants / jotnar, titans, landwights, etc.

If you'd like to read about some of my approaches you could check out these articles of mine on my website HeathenSoulLore.net, under the menu heading "Wights & Spirits."

"Landwights and Human Ecology." In this article I suggest a number of approaches for working with landwights or land-spirits with the understanding that we are co-actors within ecosystems that include natural, human, and metaphysical beings, systems, and energies interacting together.

"Renewable Energy Installations as Jotun-Shrines" (Jotun refers to giants, which Stewart includes within the faery world). Here I suggest that we regard renewable energy installations, with all their pros and cons, as loci for respectful interaction between humans and giants, taking into account the potential negatives as well as positives of both giant and human behavior.

"An Anglo-Saxon Charm Against a Dwarf: Shapeshifting, Soul Theft, and Shamanic Healing," which discusses both harmful and helpful activities of dwarves as both causing and healing human illnesses, based on my analysis of an ancient healing charm.

"Dwarves and their Powers," where I offer my belief that the fundamental power of the dwarves involves extracting from ambient sources and then condensing a kind of soul-power or energy called 'Mod,' which they use to empower their works of magical craft. Humans and animals possess this soul-power / energy as well; dwarves are traditionally known to steal or 'suck' this energy from them, but we can also work with the dwarves to enhance our own Mod-power *if* we can create a cooperative relationship with them.

"The Living Jewels of Brisingamen, subsection called 'The Forging of Brisingamen.'" Brisingamen is the sacred ornament, necklace or belt of the Goddess Freya, which was forged by four dwarves. This section of the article discusses how Freya and the dwarves interacted to create Brisingamen, an example of working with faery beings.

As I said, the beings I work with have a different sort of flavor than those who are found in the more Celtic oriented traditions--the ones who seem very sort of 'aristocratic' and capricious. I don't feel much affinity with those, but the faery world, as Stewart suggests, is a widespread one which interacts differently with different human cultures and traditions. There is much to be learned from all of them, I believe!

Winifred Hodge Rose

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,


Jean Dubuis stated the following in his Fundamentals of Esoteric Knowledge:

[...] "strive to be in harmony with the egregore of your birth because you can find there what suits you best on this Earth, for the degree of evolution in which you find yourself."

Dubuis also felt you should focus on traditions that are from your own wider culture in general; not exclusively from your country of birth. He seems to have meant that in its broadest sense i.e. Western and Eastern cultures.

I was born and raised in Ireland, and in recent years I have felt very attracted to some of the native beliefs and practices of Irish culture. I was also raised as a Catholic; but this is now causing some problems for me in terms of feeling guilt about interest in esotericism.


Dubuis' opinions really got me thinking about all of this. Luc Benoist's book 'The Esoteric Path' also says something similar. Benoist also believes that you should practice the Path that your ancestors followed:


"Our inherited path for purely practical reasons is best, since we are bound to it by psychic, intellectual, and emotional ties, and our spirituality consists in the synthesis and elevation of these ties. All spiritual symbolism possesses an 'aura' which is the result of its origins and is tinged with the colour of place, time, and language. Adaptation to a new and alien way is no easier than the conscious acceptance of and a clear-minded penetration into the deeper meaning of the old. It is better to accept our own national tradition, which we are free to go more deeply into if we can. For whatever we may do from the deepest recesses of our own hearts to the darkness of the sublime, the Path, as Plotinus has said, always leads from the alone to the Alone."


Christianity has been in Ireland since the 5th Century. But traditions like Druidry and other forms of spirituality have been here for far longer. Which one do you think would be the more powerful influence? Which one would qualify as more native? I suppose it could be a combination of the two, given how long Christianity has been here. A lot of pre-Christian practices were incorporated into Christian ones.


Do you have any thoughts on all of this? Thanks in advance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I disagree with Dubuis and Benoist. Practicing an ancestral faith is doubtless an option for many people but it's simply not true to say that it's the best option for all. My ancestral religion is old-fashioned Scottish Presbyterianism, and I know of few faiths that turn my stomach more violently. My religious tradition, Druidry, was invented out of whole cloth three centuries ago by a bunch of English and Welsh eccentrics; my magical path is a jumble of influences in which Egypt, ancient Greece, and the late 19th and early 20th century Anglo-American cultural sphere are the major keys; and the one other tradition that appeals powerfully to me is Japanese esoteric Buddhism, with which I have no ancestral ties at all.

It seems to me that the fixation on ancestral traditions is an example of excessive focus on the material plane. The origins of your bodily genetics do not determine the affinities of your soul.

replies.title: Some further thoughts
replies.content:
To add to what John is saying here, I also disagree. I have felt drawn to a deity from India for quite awhile I didn't realize she had been there for some time, ticking away in the background, until much later when I connected a few dates. Recently I had an experience, where after prayer to the deity, I picked up a book on Vedanta for free from one of these "Free Little Libraries" people sometimes stick in their yards (a box of used books). Then I had an amazing dream the next night where I was at a dhrupad concert and studying the specific way the singers moved their hands. Like John, with his interest in Japanese esoteric Buddhism, I have no ancestral connection to anything from India.

It seems to me that the esoteric student of today should avail themselves of the traditions to be found around the world.

In general, also, I think for those who feel called to the path, there may come a time in your life when you need to be of service to someone of a different faith than the one you were born into. Knowing something of world religions and traditions will be of use to you if you have to act as a mage ori priest in a universal manner. In coming times too, when more and more people will once again be searching for a path of meaning, knowing something about the different options available may be useful when using discernment if you are ever called on to teach and guide those just getting started.

Finally, if you believe in reincarnation, you may have had lives in different cultures, but as you recover those memories further along the path, it will be a chance to weave those influences back together under the guidance of your eternal soul.

Justin Patrick Moore

comments.title: Suicide and karma
comments.content:
I know you've written before about the karmic consequences of suicide, but of course I can't find those comments. Would you please talk about it one more time? Particularly regarding someone who exited because of mental/emotional pain (grief, depression, anxiety, loneliness) vs. a physical reason like incurable terminal disease. Thank you!

--Smaragdine Unkempt Beaver

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content:
If you commit suicide in an attempt to run away from your problems, it won't work; they'll be waiting for you on the other side, and will accompany you into your next incarnation. In the meantime, according to many teachings, the suicide's soul will have to wait around in an intermediate state until the time when death would have normally occurred. It's as though you tried to cut ahead in line; you'll be gently but firmly made to wait until your turn comes up.

Incurable terminal disease -- that's a slightly more complex matter. A person who is in the process of dying can refuse food and water without karmic penalty; death is already in process, and this just speeds it up a bit. (The suffering of hunger and thirst that comes with the experience work off any karma that might be involved.) Anything more active? That's suicide or, if done by someone else, homicide. The same is true if there's a terminal diagnosis but the person isn't actively dying. It really is best in most situations to deal with things as they come and let the guy with the scythe show up when he chooses.

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content: Related to this. My best friend from childhood committed suicide due to depression in her mid forties a few years ago. I reckon she will be spending a long time in that in between as she was otherwise in very good health and would doubtless have lived a long life otherwise. Is there anything I can do to help ease her situation? It's been years but I miss her every day and the thought of her in that inbetween saddens me so much. Or is this akin to praying for someone without their permission?

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content: Prayer is always a good choice in that situation. Prayer benefits the dead a great deal -- they can perceive it and it affects the energetic tone of their surroundings. Because of that, it's normally considered acceptable to pray for any dead person as long as you're not trying to pressure them (say, push a god on them, or get them to reincarnate somewhere in particular). Asking a deity to bless a dead person is always acceptable, and a very welcome gift to those on the other side.

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content: Thank you, this is very comforting to know.

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content: Not OP, but I was wondering about the case where the person is comatose or on life support— essentially being kept alive only by artificial means. Would taking them off of life support be equivalent to suicide (if they requested it beforehand) or homicide or is that case also different?

replies.title: Re: Suicide and karma
replies.content: If their body is being kept going on life support, and would die promptly otherwise, they're already dead -- the body is just being kept mechanically lurching through the motions of life. Hitting the off switch is a merciful thing to do. Coma's a more complex thing, because some people revive from it. If there's no evidence of ongoing brain function, however, that's another case where letting the body die is fine because the soul is already gone.

comments.content:
Hello John.

Can discursive meditation be used to unpack topics besides esotericism? Something like understanding why this or that event happened in history or fiction.

Do you think Crowley was familiar with using discursive meditation to acquire the knowledge to unpack occult wisdom? He wrote about scrying the Enochian Athyrs, meditation and pathworking, all topics of discursive meditation. If so, why are his teachings welcomed only in Thelema and not anywhere in the Golden Dawn where he continues to remain a highly disliked and controversial figure?

You told the person who asked about discursive meditation on angels that it's not as abstract as it looks and can even put you in contact with the angel or its subordinate. First, how is this known?

Second, can this be done at will? Say I wish to know if an angel of Capricorn (10th house) can reveal to me insights about the 10th house.


-Janick

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, it can.

2) No, Crowley never learned discursive meditation -- it might have done him a lot of good if he had. In his childhood, very nearly the only people in Britain who were still practicing it were High Church Anglicans, and the sect his parents belonged to -- the Plymouth Brethren, the church that invented modern Christian fundamentalism -- was hostile to anything and everything High Church. So he learned nothing about meditation until he started studying Asian spiritual traditions (or rather the versions of those that had been bowdlerized for Western consumption), which taught only the mind-emptying kinds of meditation.

As for why Crowley's teachings weren't accepted outside of his own circle of followers, a lot of it's because he was personally a jerk. Also, his claim to be the Prophet of a new Aeon doesn't impress most people -- quite the contrary. Crowley never grasped that an Aeon is not a period of time, it's an eternal spiritual possibility, and every mage speaks the Word of whatever Aeon his or her own soul is affiliated with, so he wasn't doing anything special in speaking the Word of his particular Aeon. He badly needed to get over himself. Since people quite rightly look at the personalities of practitioners of any system of spiritual training and ask themselves, "Do I really want to become more like that?" -- well, not a lot of people want to become more like Aleister Crowley!

3) No, it can't be done at will, if by that you mean at your will. Angels outrank you, and they -- not you -- decide when to do this.

comments.title: Two (and a half) questions

comments.title: Thinking about thinking
comments.content: Good evening,  Your response to a question I had last week made me realize I don’t fully understand the concept of the mental sheath/body/plane. How is the mental plane different from what we consider our thoughts and mind?  Thank you-Croatoan

replies.title: Re: Thinking about thinking
replies.content:
What we call thinking is a combination of two different planes. Suppose that you don't know a word of German, and I taught you the word Backpfeifengesicht without telling you what it means. You could learn to say it out loud or in your mind, you could learn to write it or to imagine it in print. When you say it in your mind or imagine writing it, that's an astral activity.

Now suppose I explain to you that it means "a face that just begs to be punched." You immediately associate it with people you know who can be described that way. It becomes a frame of meaning that links together various astral experiences (the sound of the word, the shapes of the letters, the face of that annoying jerk at work). The shared meaning is a mental activity.

As human beings, we all have the astral side of thinking, but we vary in how much we can do on the mental side. Many people can't understand the more abstract mental relations at all. That shows the differences in how far they've evolved the mental sheath toward a mental body.

Have you experienced this? If so, any chance you could explain what this might be? I usually do my altar time in the evening after a long day, so that might explain part of it, but then it goes away as soon as I step away, so not really. I can meditate on it, assign it a meaning, ask the gods, experiment, but I was curious if you had experienced this and what you thought of it if so.

As always, blessings on you for your patient guidance!

-Myriam

comments.title: Flow state
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

In western occultism, is there an analogous term for what modern psychology calls being in a flow state?

My understanding is that eastern traditions talk about this quite a bit, as with the no-mind state of Zen Buddism.

replies.title: Re: Flow state
replies.content: There are various states of higher consciousness discussed in the literature, but never in detail -- the concern seems to be that if you explain to students what they might feel, they can fake it to themselves. Western occult literature focuses instead on saying, "Do these practices and see what happens."

replies.title: Re: Flow state
replies.content:
Interesting. I hadn't thought of the concern about fooling oneself. Now I'm seeing that the admonition "to be silent" applies to the teacher as it does to the student.

The concern I have with flow state is the line between absorption and distraction. There have been times where I am singularly focused on solving business problems for example, and while it feels good to be in the zone, the end result is really mundane--not a higher state of consciousness!

I tried mapping flow state to the tarot and what I came up with was the negative aspect was the seven of cups (getting carried away and distracted by mental constructs) and the positive aspect was eight of pentacles (absorption in productive tasks).

Thank you very much for the time you put into hosting this forum. It is a really valuable resource for many people and I have learned a great deal by reading your responses to everyone's questions.

replies.title: Re: Flow state
replies.content: The thing that interests me about the discussion of "flow state" is that it seems to be specific to those branches of Asian spirituality that have close ties to the martial arts. Zen, for example, has a lot to say about it, but Japanese esoteric Buddhism -- which didn't have that much of a martial arts dimension -- does not. Thus I tend to think that it's practically useful but not necessarily spiritually useful (except insofar as not dying in battle makes it easier to continue your meditations...)

comments.title: New Trend Among Gen Z
comments.content:
Thank you for hosting these Magic Mondays and taking our questions.

Mr. Greer,
I'd like to draw your attention to a trend among Gen Z that I discovered recently (in case you have not already commented upon it): the practice of "reality shifting" to one's "desired reality": https://www.dazeddigital.com/life-culture/article/62908/1/reality-shifting-why-zoomers-are-choosing-to-transcend-material-reality

Essentially, these young people believe that they can shift their consciousness to "alternate realities", which vary between tropical locations to popular media like Harry Potter or Marvel. They do this often by first entering "void state", a state of pure awareness where they "leave the physical plane."

I discussed this with an advanced tantric practitioner and he said to me, "Oh, they're creating mind temples. We use them in Tantra as a substitute for physical objects and spaces, and to interact with the deities." He further commented that one sample of "reality shifting" advice was "90% excellent and the other 10% isn't bad" at least for the purposes of strengthening visualization. We also discussed where this trend has come from and where it might go (there's a large and active reddit page devoted to it, among other things).

As a practitioner in various kinds of Western Occultism (as opposed to my friend's Indian training and take), what do you make of it?

replies.title: Re: New Trend Among Gen Z
replies.content: Israel Regardie called it "astral tourism." It can be useful as a way of building the ability to visualize, but it can also become a way of running away from one's problems.

replies.title: Re: New Trend Among Gen Z
replies.content:
I think the vast majority of practitioners will stop there. Given the direction the world is heading in, I'm very curious to see what happens with the few who decide to go farther. I'd guess several cults and 1 or 2 serious religious movements.

What I'm watching out for is when any sign that A)practitioners start bumping into each other, and B)Practitioners bumping into beings who openly declare themselves to be something completely outside the worldview of said practitioner. Should be interesting.

replies.title: Re: New Trend Among Gen Z
replies.content: I also want to see whether practitioners begin to realize that they can use the capacity for concentration and visualization for other purposes, such as ceremonial magic.

There are two main currents of life force flowing through the world we experience, the solar current (which descends from the sun) and the telluric current (which rises up from the earth). Standing stones channel the solar current down into the earth, and trees channel the telluric current up into the sky. Putting up a standing stone is a form of earth acupuncture, and if it's done in the wrong place, yes, it can mess up the flow of energy through the land. The best approach is to find a good dowser (or learn how to dowse yourself) and find a place that needs an influx of solar current; put a stone there, and it will become a source of healing and fertility for the whole neighborhood. comments.title: EMW
comments.content:
Good evening JMG and thank you for hosting this.

I would appreciate any feedback on the below, and from the commentariat if any are willing!

I have just started reading The Earth Mysteries Workbook and it brought to mind a few experiences I’ve had regarding a property (very small cattle farm) that has been in my family for three generations now.

It is my general understanding that this property was part of a Native American Reservation at one point and became available as they were pushed by the Govt to another area nearby. This is new information to me within the last year, btw. I’m not even sure if we were the first to own it after they were moved to a different area. That said, I have always felt at peace on this land, and my family has treated it in what seems a respectful manner, so maybe no bad karma considering the situation. (At least for us.)

A few years ago I visited and spent some time alone out on the property and had a very strong impression that the spirit of that land was asking for a guardian. For me to be the guardian. I had never considered the possibility that area had a spirit of sorts or that it might need a “guardian?” I was caught off guard to put it lightly. I wouldn’t say I shrugged it off, but I certainly was cautious about the experience. I didn’t really understand what that meant, or if I could even do it. Questions like, “Why me?” “Are you sure about that, that you want me to do this?” “I may be crazy for real.” came to mind immediately.

Last year I had the same, but much stronger experience there. Again, caught off guard by the experience as I had honestly “forgotten” about it. (Pushed it to the back of my mind is more like it.) It wasn’t demanding, or anything like that. Completely friendly as far as I could tell. I find it odd that I would be asked to fill that role. I have no means to acquire the property, I don’t really want to work it, (I’m no spring chicken anymore) and I almost couldn’t live farther away from there at this point which means I only get out there once a year at most. Currently it seems like an almost impossible ask/task.

I suppose my questions would be:

1) Has anything similar to this happened to anyone else on the forum? If so, and you are willing to share some of what “duties” this might entail? (This may be something to ask the spirit directly I suppose)

2) Am I even required to “own” the property to be it’s proper guardian. If I do need to own it, do I need to live on it?

3) Are there things I can do from a distance? (Other than paying attention to land acquisitions in the area and what developers might be planning in that area as well. Things that could negatively affect it. Sending blessings.)

4) I imagine it is very possible that I wouldn’t be the only one to have been asked to do this. Is that a possibility? I ask this only because someone else may be in a much better position to take on this role. Although… I was asked twice. Hmmm….

5) I appreciate any other feedback anyone is willing to offer. What am I missing that is most important about this experience?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: EMW
replies.content:
One of the things that human beings can do is act as guardian to a piece of land. You don't have to accept, but it would be a good thing to do, just as it's good karma to take care of any other living thing. You might want to go onto the piece of land and ask the land spirit what exactly it has in mind, what that would require of you, and how you should go about doing it.

Be aware that the spirit may also be talking about something you will do after your current life ends. It used to be fairly common for people now and then to step out of the cycle of incarnations for a while to take on this kind of duty, and once population levels start falling I expect that to become more common again.

replies.title: Re: EMW
replies.content:
Thank you for this.

I will be traveling there fairly soon and will make preparations to do as you've suggested.Other than the EMW, are there other texts I should/could read about sites or acting as a guardian to this piece of land. Other preparations? I suppose I'm getting into territory where the spirit itself comes into play again.

You blew my mind with the second part of your response. That possibility had never occurred to me and now I will need to spend some time thinking about that aspect as well. Perhaps it is not one or the other, but maybe an "internship" to see if I would be a good candidate for the "full-time job". Much to consider.

Thank you again!

replies.title: Re: EMW
replies.content: There's a lot of earth mysteries literature that might be relevant, much of which is cited in the bibliography of the EMW; another book that might be well worth reading is RJ Stewart's The UnderWorld Initiation. Other than that, let the earth spirit guide you!

replies.title: Re: EMW
replies.content:
Again, thank you. I added "The UnderWorld Initiation" to my already long list of "want-to-reads" based on the posts above and will move it to the top. I will also see what jumps out at me in the EMW bibliography.

I will say, for as long as I have been following MM posts, it has never once been dull. Quite the contrary. There is always something new to explore, and most of the time I never would have even thought about a particular subject otherwise.

replies.title: Re: EMW
replies.content: I'm delighted to hear this!

comments.content:
I am the person who cured insomnia by completely removing artificial light from my home. I have had two odd things that have me wondering if there might be something very odd at work with our artificial schedules. Some of it is physical, and will be worth discussing on a different forum, but one issue does seem to be worth discussing here.

My ability to hear the gods when praying (and when I am not but they want to say something) has dramatically increased, as my ability to meditate. I'm also reading a book called The Disenchanted Night that talks about the history of how artificial light altered society. One of the odder parts is a long discussion of how the educated and comfortable classes from the 16th century to the introduction of electric lighting in the early 20th century made a big deal of staying up overnight; a time which corresponds surprisingly well to when these same comfortable classes began to reject spiritual experience; and there was a massive collapse in a lot of religions around the dawn of the 20th century, and a turn away from religion being how humans handle religious experience, even among a lot of religious people. Putting both of these together, I have formed a tentative hypothesis that there is a link: somehow, staying up through part of the night on a regular basis damages our ability to connect to the spiritual world. It does not eliminate it, but it seems to make it harder.

I have formulated a few ways to test this hypothesis, and will report back later.

a) I'd like to know if you can think of any mechanism at work here, because based on the occult lore I know I am unsure why this connection would exist beyond issues with sleep deprivation in general.

b) I also wonder now if this is part of why electric lights are pushed so hard. I know plenty of people who hate them, and have seen in the news about things such as city councils being overruled by higher levels of government; there is an insane amount of funding for them as well. It all seemed odd, but it would make a lot of sense if somehow staying up through even part of the night on a regular basis changes our relationship with the spiritual world, given how many people seem oddly terrified of the spiritual side of existence...
